executive_summary: EUR/USD trades at 1.1613, down 2.2% over the past month and 3.4% from the January 2026 high of 1.2019, as the US-Israeli military strikes on Iran have triggered a classic risk-off USD bid that has overwhelmed the euro's structurally improved fundamental story. Over the past year, EUR/USD has appreciated 6.4%, driven by Germany's historic €500bn infrastructure fund, the ECB's completed easing cycle to 2.00%, and narrowing UST-Bund spreads. The ECB NEER (EER-40) at 129.5 and a REER near 97.7 suggest fair-to-modestly-cheap valuation, particularly against USD purchasing power parity of ~1.30. The central tension is between a powerful medium-term fiscal-and-growth narrative (German rearmament, manufacturing inflection, Fed-ECB convergence) and a near-term energy shock that has repriced ECB rate expectations from on hold indefinitely to two hikes by December 2026. The February HICP overshoot (1.9% headline, 2.4% core, 3.4% services) reinforces the hawkish repricing while simultaneously threatening the growth recovery that underpins the strategic bull case. Tactically bearish EUR with low conviction as energy uncertainty, positioning unwinds, and the March 18-19 ECB super-week dominate; strategically bullish with moderate conviction as the fiscal expansion and policy convergence thesis remains intact provided the Iran shock proves transitory.

spot.convention: Rising EUR/USD = EUR strengthening; falling EUR/USD = EUR weakening
spot.primary_index: ECB Nominal Effective Exchange Rate (EER-40) at 129.5 (6 March 2026), down from February average of 130.69

1m.direction: EUR weakening — Iran shock, USD safe-haven bid

3m.direction: EUR weakening — reversal from January highs

6m.direction: EUR strengthening — fiscal stimulus, ECB easing cycle

1y.direction: EUR strengthening — structural USD weakness, German fiscal shift

bis_reer.interpretation: The ECB real effective exchange rate at 97.7 sits near the long-run average, placing the euro in the 45th-55th percentile of its 20-year range — essentially fair value on a broad trade-weighted basis.

valuation.trade_weighted_assessment: The euro is broadly fairly valued on a trade-weighted basis, with clear pockets of cheapness against the USD and CHF and expensiveness against Scandinavians and commodity currencies where PPP deviations have persisted for years.
valuation.what_is_priced_in: Current valuation reflects the market's view that the eurozone growth gap with the US has narrowed but not closed, that German fiscal expansion is partially priced but not fully capitalised, and that energy risk premium deserves a discount to fundamental fair value. The PPP gap to USD has narrowed from -18% in September 2024 to -10.7% today — the trend is toward fair value but the move is only half complete.
valuation.actionability: The valuation signal is supportive strategically (EUR cheap vs USD) but not a tactical driver. PPP deviations can persist for years. The signal reinforces the direction of the strategic thesis but does not determine timing.

cftc.percentile_context: Net speculative positioning at +136,500 contracts sits in the 70th-85th percentile of the three-year range, having fallen from near-record levels in mid-February.

positioning_summary.other_positioning_signals: EUR/USD 25-delta risk reversals shifted from +0.3 vols (EUR calls bid) pre-conflict to -0.6 vols (EUR puts bid), reflecting aggressive demand for EUR downside protection. Put/call ratios are elevated as corporates and asset managers hedge EUR exposure.
positioning_summary.interpretation: Asset managers cut 25,000 contracts in a single week — the most aggressive weekly reduction in months. The 136,500 net speculative long remains elevated but clearly unwinding. In mid-February, gross longs were at fresh three-year highs. The direction of travel is unambiguously toward liquidation, with an estimated 60,000-80,000 contracts of additional selling potential before positioning reaches neutral territory.
positioning_summary.contrarian_assessment: Positioning remains a headwind for EUR bulls over the tactical horizon. At the 70th-85th percentile, substantial room exists for further unwinding before positioning becomes neutral or contrarian-bullish. Leveraged funds' modest +29,632 net long is close enough to zero that a flip to net short is a realistic near-term scenario, which would amplify downside pressure.

market_structure_vol_summary.trend_regime: Transitioning from bullish trend (Oct 2024 – Jan 2026, 1.04 to 1.20) to corrective pullback within the broader uptrend

moving_averages.alignment: Transitioning from stacked bullish to tangled — 50-DMA falling toward 100-DMA, both above 200-DMA

volatility.rv_vs_iv: RV 12.5% vs IV 11.5% — market underpricing realised moves by 1.0 pts despite post-conflict vol surge
volatility.risk_reversal_25d_1m: -0.6 vols, EUR puts bid — shifted from +0.3 pre-conflict; market aggressively hedging EUR downside
volatility.vol_regime: Elevated, event-driven — shift from low-vol trending to high-vol uncertain regime

market_structure_vol_summary.key_observation: The IV-RV dynamic is the critical structural signal: realised vol exceeds implied despite the post-conflict surge, indicating the market may still be underpricing actual EUR/USD movement. Combined with backwardated term structure pricing the ECB/Fed super-week, options are expensive at the front end but may be cheap relative to actual event risk.

market_context.recent_regime: EUR/USD spent January-February 2026 consolidating near 1.18-1.20, supported by the German fiscal expansion narrative, manufacturing PMI improvement, and broad USD weakness driven by the February NFP shock (-92,000) and US stagflation fears. The regime shifted abruptly on 28 February when US-Israeli strikes on Iran triggered a risk-off USD bid, TTF gas price spike, and immediate repricing of ECB rate expectations. The current regime is characterised by high event risk, elevated but declining positioning, and a fundamental tug-of-war between the structural fiscal tailwind and the cyclical energy headwind. Price action is dominated by geopolitical headlines (intraday swings of 100+ pips) rather than data flow, making the tactical environment exceptionally uncertain.

base_currency.expected_action: Hold (hike risk rising — ~20% probability of 25bp hike)

key_spreads.interpretation: Bund 129bps below UST; compressed from ~170bps in H2 2025, narrowing supports EUR/USD
Bund 175bps below Gilt; widened on UK stagflation, favours EUR vs GBP
Bund 67bps above JGB; EUR carry advantage vs JPY, though narrowing as BoJ hikes
BTP-Bund spread at 76bps, near multi-year tights; Italy risk contained
OAT-Bund at 77bps; France converging with Italy, reflecting fiscal deterioration
Bonos-Bund at 47bps; Spain outperforming on stronger growth

sovereign_yields.curve_shape: Bund 2s10s at +47bps, positive and steepening on fiscal expansion and rising term premium from increased Bund supply

tactical.analysis: The ECB deposit rate at 2.00% sits 9th of 11 in the G10+SGD ranking, 163bps below the Fed funds rate midpoint and 175bps below the BoE bank rate, firmly establishing EUR as a funding currency alongside JPY and CHF. The rate differential landscape had been expected to narrow in EUR's favour through H1 2026 as the Fed cut and the ECB held, but the Iran energy shock has disrupted this calculus from both sides. Markets now price ~55% probability of an ECB hike by July and ~85% by December, representing a dramatic swing from the pre-conflict expectation of no change through 2027. The March 18-19 meeting with updated staff projections is the single most consequential event of the tactical horizon. Lagarde's 10 March hawkish escalation warning the ECB will prevent a repeat of the 2022-23 inflation surge suggests the Governing Council is considering a pre-emptive pivot. However, Lane's earlier framing of the shock as potentially a 0.8pp inflation add and 0.6pp growth drag implies the ECB sees this as a stagflationary impulse, not a pure demand shock, which argues for caution rather than immediate tightening. The Fed meeting on 18 March adds to tactical uncertainty — a dovish Fed would narrow the rate differential and support EUR regardless of the ECB outcome. The Bund 2Y at 2.39% has surged 34bps in one month while the UST-Bund 10Y spread at ~129bps has compressed from ~170bps in late 2025.
key_data_points: ECB deposit rate: 2.00%, 9th of 11 in G10+SGD
Fed funds rate: 3.50-3.75% (midpoint 3.625), +163bps above ECB
German Bund 2Y: 2.39%, up 34bps in one month
German Bund 10Y: 2.86%, highest since March 2025
UST-Bund 10Y spread: ~129bps, compressed from ~170bps in H2 2025
OIS-implied: ~55% probability of hike by July, ~85% by December
Market pricing: two full 25bp hikes to 2.50% by end-2026
Fed pricing: ~1 cut in H2 2026
tactical.what_is_priced: The market has priced a near-complete reversal of the ECB on hold narrative, now assigning ~85% probability to two hikes by December. This feels aggressive given the energy shock may prove transitory and the ECB has consistently signalled data dependence. If the ECB holds and pushes back on hike expectations at the March meeting, the front end would rally and EUR could weaken on rate repricing.

strategic.analysis: Over the strategic horizon, the rate differential trajectory strongly favours EUR. The Fed is more likely to cut than hike (stagflation dynamics, -92,000 NFP, unemployment at 4.4%), while the ECB at most hikes 25-50bps, meaning the spread narrows from +163bps toward +100-125bps. This spread compression is the primary driver of EUR/USD's 6.4% appreciation over the past year and the mechanism underpinning the consensus forecast of 1.20+ by year-end. Germany's fiscal expansion fundamentally alters the eurozone growth trajectory that had justified the wide rate differential. DIW estimates the fiscal impulse will boost GDP by 1% in 2026 and 2% annually from 2027, narrowing the US-eurozone growth gap. The Bund curve has steepened meaningfully (2s10s at +47bps) as markets price fiscal expansion increasing term premium. If the Iran shock proves transitory, the rate differential narrative reasserts itself with the added tailwind of German fiscal stimulus. A higher-growth, higher-inflation eurozone is structurally EUR-positive because it argues for a neutral rate above 2.00% and attracts capital inflows. The 10Y Bund-JGB spread at +67bps and Gilt-Bund spread widening to +166-188bps both favour EUR in cross terms.
key_data_points: UST-Bund 10Y spread trajectory: 170bps to 129bps over 6M, expected to reach 100-110bps
German fiscal expansion: €500bn infrastructure fund + defence at 3.5% of GDP by 2029
DIW GDP boost estimate: +1% in 2026, +2% annually from 2027
ECB Dec 2025 projections: GDP 1.2%, HICP 1.9%, Core HICP 2.2% for 2026
Bund 2s10s: +47bps, steepening on fiscal expansion
Fed dot plot median: 3.25% for end-2026
Consensus EUR/USD year-end: 1.20 (Reuters poll median), range 1.10-1.25
strategic.what_is_priced: The strategic rate path is partially priced. Markets have repriced the ECB from done cutting to possibly hiking, which compresses the forward differential faster than pre-conflict. However, the Fed side is not yet fully priced — if the US labour market deterioration continues, the Fed may cut more aggressively than currently priced, creating additional spread compression. The edge is in the Fed's reaction function: the market is pricing ~1 Fed cut when the labour data argues for 2-3.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical neutral vs strategic bullish reflects the genuine binary uncertainty of the March 18-19 super-week. The ECB's response to the Iran shock could either validate the hawkish repricing or push back against it. The strategic view takes precedence once the ECB reveals its hand — by April, the path forward will be clearer and the tactical and strategic stances should converge.

carry_vs_peers.interpretation: Negative carry; EUR is a funding currency vs USD
Negative carry; EUR funds GBP longs
Positive carry vs JPY; moderate attractiveness
Best carry trade; EUR/CHF carry is the most attractive EUR cross
Negative carry; EUR funds AUD longs

carry_analysis.carry_regime: EUR is a funding currency in the current regime, sitting alongside JPY and CHF at the bottom of the G10 carry ranking. The only pairs where EUR earns positive carry are vs JPY (+125bps) and CHF (+200bps), with EUR/CHF offering the most attractive carry-to-vol ratio at +0.25.
carry_analysis.breakeven_move: Against USD, EUR/USD would need to appreciate ~163 pips annualised to breakeven on carry — approximately 41 pips per quarter or ~14 pips per month. Given 3M IV of ~10.0%, carry is a rounding error relative to directional risk.

tactical.analysis: The February HICP flash estimate delivered a significant upside surprise: headline at 1.9% (up from 1.7% in January, above consensus 1.7%), core at 2.4% (up from 2.2%), and services at 3.4% (up from 3.2%). This re-acceleration demolishes the inflation problem solved narrative. Services inflation at 3.4% is particularly troublesome — at 42% of the HICP basket, it must decelerate to ~2.0-2.5% for headline to sustainably hit the 2% target. The energy shock from Iran has not yet appeared in the February data, meaning the March and April HICP prints will capture the Brent surge above $90 and TTF doubling, potentially pushing headline to 2.3-2.5% or higher. On the growth side, the February Composite PMI at 51.9 and first manufacturing expansion since June 2022 (PMI 50.8) were encouraging but pre-date the Iran shock. German factory orders plunged -11.1% MoM in January. The net assessment is bearish EUR tactically because the inflation data argues for tighter policy while simultaneously threatening the nascent growth recovery.
key_data_points: HICP headline: 1.9% YoY (Feb flash), up from 1.7% (Jan), above consensus 1.7%
HICP core: 2.4% YoY (Feb), up from 2.2% (Jan)
Services inflation: 3.4% YoY (Feb), re-accelerating
Eurozone GDP Q4 2025: +0.2% QoQ, +1.2% YoY
February Composite PMI: 51.9, 14th consecutive expansion month
February Manufacturing PMI: 50.8, first expansion in 44 months
German factory orders Jan 2026: -11.1% MoM
ECB negotiated wages (forward 2026): 2.4%, easing from 3.2%
tactical.what_is_priced: The market has shifted from pricing benign disinflation to pricing an energy-driven inflation resurgence. OIS markets now imply two ECB hikes by December. However, the growth side is not yet priced for deterioration — European equities bounced 2.5% on 10 March and PMIs remain in expansion territory. If the March PMI flash shows a sharp deterioration (sub-50 composite), markets would need to reprice the growth leg, creating a stagflationary setup that is unambiguously EUR-negative.

strategic.analysis: The strategic inflation-growth dynamic depends on whether the Iran shock is transitory or persistent. In the base case, inflation temporarily overshoots to 2.3-2.5% before fading back toward the ECB's 1.9% projection as energy base effects normalise. Core at 2.4% and services at 3.4% remain sticky, but the wage tracker at 2.4% suggests the wage-price spiral risk is diminishing. On growth, the strategic picture has been transformed by Germany's fiscal revolution. The €500bn infrastructure fund and uncapped defence spending represent the largest fiscal impulse in eurozone history. DIW estimates +1% GDP in 2026 and +2% annually from 2027. The February manufacturing PMI at 50.8 may be an early signal of fiscal transmission. Deutsche Bank forecasts German industrial production up 2-3% in 2026, the first increase since 2021. Eurozone unemployment at a record low 6.1% provides a buffer against energy-driven slowdown. The combination of fiscal expansion, record-low unemployment, and manufacturing recovery argues for eurozone GDP trending toward 1.5-1.8% in 2027.
key_data_points: ECB Dec 2025 projections: HICP 1.9%, core 2.2%, GDP 1.2% for 2026
ECB wage tracker (forward-looking 2026): 2.4%, easing from 3.2% in 2025
German fiscal impulse: €500bn infrastructure + 3.5% GDP defence by 2029
DIW growth boost: +1% GDP in 2026, +2% from 2027
Eurozone unemployment: 6.1%, record low
German manufacturing PMI: 50.8, first expansion in 44 months
Full-year 2025 eurozone GDP: 1.4%, up from 0.9% in 2024
strategic.what_is_priced: Consensus GDP for the eurozone in 2026 is ~1.2%. The German fiscal expansion is not fully reflected in these forecasts — the March staff projections will provide the first comprehensive assessment. If the fiscal multiplier delivers even half of DIW's 1% estimate, actual GDP could print 1.5-1.7%, representing a meaningful upside surprise. The bond market has partially priced this via Bund curve steepening, but equities and FX have not fully capitalised the growth uplift.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical bearish vs strategic bullish reflects the timing mismatch between the immediate energy shock (negative for growth, ambiguous for rates) and the structural fiscal expansion (positive for growth over quarters). The February inflation overshoot dominates the 2-4 week horizon; the €500bn infrastructure fund and manufacturing inflection dominate the 1-6 month horizon.

tactical.analysis: The risk environment has deteriorated sharply since the Iran conflict erupted on 28 February. The VIX spiked to 29.5 on 6 March before easing to ~23-25, well above the pre-conflict sub-15. European equities have sold off from all-time highs — Euro Stoxx 50 dropped 6.2% from 6,200 to ~5,818, DAX fell 6.2% from 25,508 to ~23,917. European bank equities sold off 12% from ATH on the STOXX 600 Banks index. Credit spreads widened meaningfully: iTraxx Europe Main from sub-55bps to 57-61bps, Crossover from ~260 to ~270bps with the 3 March 11bp single-day widening being the largest since October. EUR is not a safe haven — it typically weakens in risk-off as capital flows to USD, CHF, and JPY. The current risk-off is amplified for EUR by the energy channel: Hormuz closure directly threatens European energy security with EU gas storage at 30%. EUR's anti-dollar character means USD strength on risk-off directly weakens EUR due to its 57.6% DXY weight.
key_data_points: VIX: ~23-25, down from 29.5 peak; pre-conflict sub-15
Euro Stoxx 50: ~5,818, -6.2% from ATH (6,200)
STOXX 600 Banks: ~334, -12% from ATH (385)
iTraxx Europe Main: ~57-61bps, up from sub-55
iTraxx Crossover: ~270bps, +11bps single-day on 3 March
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets are pricing a partial de-escalation (10 March equity bounce), but risk indicators remain elevated. If the Hormuz closure persists beyond March, or if Iran retaliates further, equities would retest the 6 March lows and EUR/USD could test the 200-DMA at 1.1412. A severe risk-off (VIX >35, Crossover >350bps) is not priced.

strategic.analysis: Over the strategic horizon, risk appetite is a low-weight factor (0.05) and direction depends on Iran conflict resolution. In the base case (de-escalation over weeks), risk indicators normalise toward pre-conflict levels: VIX sub-15, credit spreads near tights, equity indices recovering. This would be EUR-neutral-to-positive as the anti-dollar relationship reasserts. The structural risk landscape has shifted with European defence spending commitments (€800bn ReArm Europe) creating both a risk response and growth driver. The US economy's vulnerability (NFP -92,000, GDP +1.4% SAAR) introduces a new channel — if the US enters recession, the dollar smile framework suggests initial USD strength then USD weakness as the Fed cuts aggressively. EUR benefits in the second phase. European bank equities, despite the 12% pullback, are up 31% YoY with intact fundamentals. The tail risk is the Iran conflict broadening into a sustained Middle East war.
key_data_points: VIX 6M average pre-conflict: ~13-15; current ~24
European defence commitment: €800bn ReArm Europe plan
STOXX Banks YoY: +31% despite 12% pullback
US NFP Feb: -92,000; US unemployment: 4.4%
US GDP Q4 SAAR: +1.4%
EUR risk beta (6M): ~0.5-0.7 to global risk-on
strategic.what_is_priced: Equity markets are pricing a moderate, transitory disruption rather than sustained escalation. If this proves correct, the risk backdrop is neutral-to-supportive for EUR. Current credit spreads suggest the market assigns ~20-25% probability to a prolonged disruption.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical bearish at 70% confidence vs strategic neutral at 50% reflects the dominance of the acute risk-off environment over 2-4 weeks vs the mean-reverting tendency of risk indicators over 1-6 months. If the conflict de-escalates rapidly, the tactical stance would converge to neutral within days.

tactical.analysis: CFTC positioning as of 3 March shows speculative EUR longs have begun unwinding but remain elevated. Non-commercial net longs at +136,500 contracts sit in the 70th-85th percentile of the three-year range, having fallen from near-record levels in mid-February. Asset managers cut 25,000 contracts in a single week, the most aggressive weekly reduction in months. Leveraged funds reduced by 7,165 to a modest +29,632 net long. The positioning overhang is the clearest bearish signal for the tactical horizon — with asset managers still holding €46.3bn notional in EUR longs, the potential for further liquidation is substantial. Dealer intermediaries are massively net short (-454,680 contracts) as counterparty to real-money longs. Options positioning has shifted decisively: 25-delta risk reversals moved from +0.3 to -0.6 vols. The positioning unwind creates a self-reinforcing dynamic: as EUR weakens, stop-losses trigger further liquidation. This typically exhausts at neutral territory (~40th percentile), which is approximately 60,000-80,000 contracts of additional selling from current levels.
key_data_points: Non-commercial net: +136,500 contracts (~70th-85th percentile 3Y)
Asset managers: +370,272 contracts (€46.3bn notional), -25,029 WoW
Leveraged funds: +29,632 contracts (€3.7bn notional), -7,165 WoW
Open interest: 913,289 contracts
25d risk reversal 1M: -0.6 vols (EUR puts bid), was +0.3 pre-conflict
Estimated distance to neutral: 60,000-80,000 contracts
Dealer intermediaries: -454,680 net short
tactical.what_is_priced: The positioning unwind is partially priced — EUR/USD has already fallen 3.4% from the January high. But with net specs still in the 70th-85th percentile, the market is betting the unwind is orderly. A catalyst for accelerated liquidation (ECB dovish surprise, Hormuz closure extension, EUR/USD break below 200-DMA) could trigger a positioning cascade that is not priced.

strategic.analysis: Over 1-6 months, positioning is a lower-weight factor and direction depends on whether the current unwind creates a contrarian opportunity or new trend. If EUR longs unwind to neutral over the next 4-8 weeks, this removes the positioning headwind and creates a cleaner slate for the structural bull case. Historical analogues suggest EUR positioning mean-reverts from extremes within 2-3 months. Reserve manager flows provide a modest structural bid: COFER data shows EUR's share at 20.33% in Q3 2025, from 19.8% at end-2024, though the pace is glacial. ECB balance sheet normalisation (APP and PEPP runoff ~€500bn in 2026) creates a modest drag through excess liquidity but is known and priced. The strategic positioning outlook is neutral — neither tailwind nor headwind — with the possibility of turning bullish if the unwind overshoots to net short.
key_data_points: Historical mean-reversion: EUR positioning extremes normalise within 2-3 months
EUR share of global reserves (COFER Q3 2025): 20.33%
ECB balance sheet: €6,293bn; QT returning ~€500bn in 2026
Excess reserves: ~€2,500bn, well above pre-COVID
Open interest: elevated at 913K
Reserve manager demand: ~$5-10bn per quarter via COFER reallocation
strategic.what_is_priced: The market expects an orderly positioning unwind over 1-3 months. If the unwind is faster (panic liquidation) or slower (dip-buyers stabilise longs), the outcome differs meaningfully. The most likely scenario is gradual unwind to neutral over 6-8 weeks.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical bearish at high confidence (75%) vs strategic neutral (55%) reflects the immediate positioning headwind from elevated longs unwinding. The tactical stance is the highest-conviction factor call in this dossier — the positioning dynamics are mechanistic and well-supported by data.
factors.contrarian_analysis_tactical: Positioning is stretched long at the 70th-85th percentile of the three-year range. The unwind trigger has materialised (Iran shock, energy prices), and liquidation is underway at ~20,000-25,000 contracts per week. A positioning cascade is a meaningful risk if EUR/USD breaks the 200-DMA at 1.1412, triggering systematic trend-follower stop-losses. Historical analogues (March 2022 Russia-Ukraine, September 2022 energy crisis) saw EUR positioning swing from net long to net short within 6-8 weeks, with EUR/USD declining 5-8%. The other side of the trade: the short EUR argument is that the energy shock reprices the ECB reaction function from dovish pause to hawkish pivot, undermining the growth recovery while widening the negative carry differential.
factors.contrarian_analysis_strategic: Over 1-6 months, the contrarian picture flips. If the current unwind drives positioning to neutral or net short (100,000-150,000 contract move from mid-February peaks), this creates conditions for a powerful contrarian long. The structural EUR fundamentals (fiscal expansion, growth recovery, rate convergence) have not changed — the energy shock is a disruption, not a structural break. Historical precedent: after the 2022 energy crisis drove EUR positioning to extreme shorts, EUR/USD subsequently rallied from 0.96 to 1.12 in four months. The contrarian opportunity depends on the depth of the current unwind: deeper unwind creates a better entry point.

tactical.analysis: The eurozone's external balance has been dealt a body blow by the Iran energy shock. The current account surplus narrowed from €407bn (2.7% of GDP) in 2024 to €255bn (1.6% of GDP) in 2025. The Strait of Hormuz closure and Qatar LNG disruption sent energy prices surging: Brent hit $119.50/bbl, TTF doubled to ~€49-56/MWh, and EU gas storage sits at just ~30% — the lowest since winter 2021-22. Europe's energy import dependency means every $10/bbl increase in Brent costs ~€40-50bn annually. At $93/bbl vs ~$75 pre-conflict, the annual energy import bill increases ~€70-90bn. TTF at €50/MWh vs ~€28 pre-conflict is even more consequential for industrial competitiveness. The EU-US trade surplus widened to €247bn, creating political risk under 50% steel/aluminium tariffs and EU retaliatory tariffs on ~$28bn of US goods effective April. The terms of trade shock is the most immediate and mechanistic EUR-negative channel.
key_data_points: CA surplus 2025: €255bn (1.6% of GDP), down from €407bn (2.7%) in 2024
Brent crude: ~$93/bbl, up from ~$75 pre-conflict; touched $119.50
TTF natural gas: €49-56/MWh, doubled since 27 Feb
EU gas storage: ~30% of capacity, lowest since winter 2021-22
Energy import bill increase: ~€70-90bn annually at current prices
EU-US goods surplus: €247bn in 2025
Goods trade surplus 2025: €164.6bn
tactical.what_is_priced: The market has priced a moderate energy shock but not a prolonged disruption. If Hormuz remains closed through April, the terms of trade deterioration would be substantially worse, and the 2022 analogue (EUR/USD 1.15 to 0.96) becomes relevant. The base case of swift resolution is ~55-60% priced.

strategic.analysis: The strategic external balance depends critically on energy price resolution. In the base case (Hormuz reopens, Brent settles at $80-90, TTF normalises to €35-45/MWh), the CA surplus stabilises at ~€200-250bn (1.3-1.6% of GDP), structurally supportive though weaker than 2024 peak. Europe's energy diversification since 2022 — LNG infrastructure, reduced Russian dependency — has improved resilience, but storage at 30% creates vulnerability through summer refill season. The Commission's 90% target by October requires massive LNG purchases that will support import spending through Q2-Q3. The goods surplus with the US faces tariff headwinds while the deficit with China widened to €177bn. EUR's reserve share at 20.33% provides a structural demand floor. The German fiscal expansion should boost investment imports near-term but improve export capacity medium-term. The external balance is a neutral factor because the pre-shock improvement trajectory has been interrupted but not reversed.
key_data_points: CA surplus trajectory: €407bn (2024) to €255bn (2025) to ~€200-250bn (2026E)
EU gas refill target: 90% by Oct 2026 (current: 30%)
EU-China goods deficit: €177bn (2025)
EU retaliatory tariffs: ~$28bn US goods, effective April 2026
EUR reserve share (COFER): 20.33%
EU energy diversification: LNG capacity expanded ~40% since 2022
strategic.what_is_priced: The market is pricing a transitory energy shock (55-60% probability). If correct, the external balance is a non-factor strategically. If disruption persists into Q2-Q3, the CA could swing to near-balance, removing a key structural EUR support — the 2022 precedent saw the CA swing from +3% to near-zero of GDP.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical bearish at 70% vs strategic neutral at 50% reflects the immediate terms of trade shock from energy prices vs the expectation that this normalises over the strategic horizon. The confidence gap is wider than most factors, reflecting genuine uncertainty about energy disruption duration.

tactical.analysis: ECB communication has shifted hawkishly since the Iran conflict, but the Governing Council has not yet formally updated guidance. The February statement described policy as in a good place with readiness to act in either direction. Lagarde's 10 March television appearance marked the most hawkish post-conflict communication, explicitly invoking the 2022-23 inflation experience. Lane (3 March) framed the shock as stagflationary rather than pure demand, arguing for caution rather than immediate action. Schnabel (6 March) warned against complacency. The Account of the February meeting revealed some members questioned if easing went too far. The divergence between Lagarde's hawkish public posturing and Lane's calibrated framing creates uncertainty about the March meeting outcome. ECB credibility is high — it successfully navigated 2022-23 with a 450bp hiking cycle. This credibility gives the ECB optionality: it can credibly signal hawkishness without acting immediately.
key_data_points: Last meeting: 4-5 Feb 2026, unanimous hold at 2.00%
Lagarde (10 March): hawkish escalation, pledged to prevent 2022-23 repeat
Lane (3 March): Iran could add +0.8pp inflation, -0.6pp growth
Schnabel (6 March): warned against complacency
February account: some members questioned if easing went too far
Market-implied: ~85% probability of two hikes by December
Gap: market prices ~2.50%, ECB guidance is data-dependent hold
tactical.what_is_priced: The market has moved significantly ahead of formal ECB guidance. Two hikes priced by December, but ECB has not endorsed any tightening path. If March produces a hawkish hold with hikes on the table language, market pricing is validated. If the ECB pushes back, the front end rallies and EUR could weaken. The gap between market pricing (~2.50% by December) and our assessment (~2.00-2.25%) suggests hike pricing is ~25bps too aggressive.

strategic.analysis: Over the strategic horizon, ECB credibility and guidance framework are EUR-supportive. The ECB's track record means that if the Governing Council shifts toward tightening, markets will take the signal seriously and front-run the move. This credibility premium effectively does part of the tightening work through financial conditions. The December 2025 Staff Projections will be revised at March — fiscal expansion should push GDP higher while energy pushes inflation higher. A higher-growth, higher-inflation eurozone is an environment the ECB can manage without aggressive action. The Governing Council's large size (25 members) creates a consensus-building dynamic that delays action but makes eventual moves durable. The quarterly projection rounds provide regular recalibration opportunities. Over the strategic horizon, the ECB's communication framework provides regular opportunities to adjust guidance incrementally, reducing sharp surprise repricing risk.
key_data_points: ECB credibility: successfully navigated 450bp hiking cycle in 2022-23
Next projection round: March meeting (18-19 March)
Expected revisions: GDP higher (fiscal), HICP higher (energy)
Governing Council size: 25 members, consensus-building
Communication: 8 meetings/year, 4 with projections
Key personnel continuity: Lagarde term until 2027
strategic.what_is_priced: The market is pricing an ECB willing to hike — partially correct but trigger threshold for actual hikes is higher than markets imply. The ECB's reaction function requires persistent above-target inflation driven by demand, not a transitory supply shock. If core inflation remains sticky at 2.4% AND growth continues, a hike by Q3 is plausible. If energy normalises and core disinflation resumes, hike expectations collapse.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical neutral vs strategic bullish reflects near-term communication uncertainty (March meeting is genuinely binary) vs strategic confidence that ECB credibility and transparent communication will ultimately support EUR through data-dependent guidance.

tactical.analysis: The US-Israeli war on Iran is the defining event risk. Strikes beginning 28 February killed Khamenei; Iran retaliated by closing Hormuz, attacking Gulf infrastructure, and disrupting Qatar LNG. Brent surged to $119.50 before Trump's de-escalation signal sent it back to ~$93. For EUR, the event operates through three channels: energy prices threatening external balance and inflation; risk-off USD bid weakening EUR; and European defence spending acceleration. The first two are acutely EUR-negative while the third is ambiguously positive. The March 18-19 super-week (Fed, ECB, BoE, SNB within 48 hours) is extraordinary event concentration. German state elections add domestic noise. EU retaliatory tariffs (~$28bn) effective April and the Turnberry health check in July add trade policy risk. The UN ceasefire resolution and Geneva talks provide diplomatic channels but no breakthrough is expected imminently.
key_data_points: Iran conflict: started 28 Feb, Khamenei killed, Hormuz closed
Brent: peaked $119.50 (9 March), settled ~$93
Trump (9 March): operations very complete — de-escalation signal
Super-week: BoJ (13 Mar), Fed/BoC (18 Mar), ECB/BoE (19 Mar), SNB (20 Mar)
EU retaliatory tariffs: ~$28bn US goods, effective April
EU defence: €800bn ReArm Europe plan
UN ceasefire resolution: 107-12-51 on 24 Feb
tactical.what_is_priced: The market is pricing moderate, contained conflict with de-escalation within weeks. A prolonged Hormuz closure or escalation is not priced — this would send Brent back above $110 and EUR/USD toward 1.12. Conversely, rapid ceasefire and Hormuz reopening would trigger a sharp risk-on rally pushing EUR/USD back above 1.18.

strategic.analysis: Over the strategic horizon, the Iran conflict's impact depends on duration and resolution. In the base case (de-escalation within weeks to low-grade tension), the acute EUR-negative impact fades and the event catalyses structural European changes: accelerated defence spending, energy diversification investments, potentially a Ukraine peace dividend. The EU's €800bn ReArm Europe plan transforms European defence posture and provides a significant fiscal impulse. EU-US trade tensions remain elevated but manageable. The Ukraine diplomacy track offers a potential positive catalyst if progress materialises — a ceasefire would significantly reduce the European energy risk premium. French political stability improved with budget adoption. The strategic event risk is balanced between upside catalysts (peace dividend, defence spending) and downside risks (escalation, trade war).
key_data_points: ReArm Europe: €800bn total
Germany defence target: 3.5% of GDP by 2029
Ukraine diplomacy: three rounds of trilateral talks, no breakthrough
EU-US tariff status: 10-15% Section 122; 50% steel/aluminium
French budget: adopted 2 Feb via Article 49.3
Estimated probability prolonged Hormuz closure (>3 months): 15-20%
strategic.what_is_priced: Market prices ~55-60% probability of swift de-escalation. A Ukraine ceasefire is not priced (assessed <20% within 6 months). If one materialises, it would be worth 2-4% EUR/USD upside. EU-US trade war escalation to autos (~10% probability) would offset much of the fiscal expansion benefit.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical bearish at 80% confidence vs strategic neutral at 45% reflects the acute nature of the live military conflict over 2-4 weeks vs the balanced upside/downside distribution over 1-6 months. The confidence gap is the widest in the dossier, reflecting radical uncertainty from a live conflict.

tactical.analysis: Eurozone fiscal dynamics are dominated by three divergent trajectories: Germany's expansion, France's deterioration, and Italy's improvement. Germany's €500bn infrastructure fund and defence spending push projected deficits to 3.5-4% of GDP — a radical shift. France received S&P and Fitch downgrades to A+ in 2025, with the 2026 budget targeting 5.0% deficit via business-unfriendly tax hikes. OAT-Bund at 70-77bps has widened from post-budget tights. Italy enjoyed upgrades from all three agencies (Moody's first in 23 years) with BTP-Bund at 76-84bps near tights. The reformed SGP is under strain with 17 countries activating the defence escape clause. Tactically, this is noise: sovereign spreads are contained and the ECB's TPI backstop remains available but unneeded.
key_data_points: Germany deficit projection: 3.5-4% of GDP
France deficit target: 5.0% (2026 budget); 2024 actual: 5.8%
France debt/GDP: 113.2%; interest bill €59.3bn in 2026
Italy deficit: 3.1% (2025); target 2.8% (2026)
BTP-Bund: 76-84bps; OAT-Bund: 70-77bps
SGP: 17 countries activated defence escape clause
tactical.what_is_priced: Sovereign spreads are well-behaved and not pricing acute stress. BTP-OAT convergence is priced and likely to persist. The German fiscal expansion is partially priced through Bund curve steepening but not through GDP growth upgrades.

strategic.analysis: Germany's fiscal revolution is the single most powerful structural EUR-positive development. The €500bn infrastructure fund represents ~12% of German GDP over 10+ years, with front-loaded spending in 2026-2028. Defence rising to 3.5% of GDP adds €100-150bn annually. France's deterioration is a headwind but not a crisis: borrowing costs remain manageable and the ECB's TPI provides an implicit backstop. Italy's improving trajectory reduces the peripheral risk discount that historically weighed on EUR. The reformed SGP provides fiscal coordination despite escape clauses. NextGenerationEU enters its final borrowing year with €90bn planned. The net assessment is bullish: German fiscal expansion is transformative, Italy is improving, and France is concerning but not yet at crisis levels.
key_data_points: German infrastructure fund: €500bn (~12% GDP over 10+ years)
Defence target: 3.5% GDP by 2029
Fiscal multiplier estimate: 0.5-1.0x for infrastructure
NextGenerationEU: €90bn issuance planned H1 2026
Italy upgrades: all three agencies in 2025
SGP reform: national escape clause for defence
strategic.what_is_priced: The German fiscal expansion is only partially priced. Bund curve steepening reflects increased supply, but growth implications are not yet in consensus GDP forecasts or equity valuations. The March ECB projections will provide the first official assessment, creating a potential positive catalyst if the GDP upgrade exceeds expectations.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical neutral vs strategic bullish reflects the lagged transmission of fiscal expansion. At 2-4 weeks, the fiscal story is background noise overshadowed by geopolitics. Over 1-6 months, the fiscal impulse begins flowing through procurement, infrastructure investment, and defence spending.

tactical.analysis: EUR/USD's market structure has deteriorated from a clean uptrend to a corrective pattern. The pair has fallen 3.4% from the 1.2019 high to 1.1613, pushing spot below the 50-DMA (1.1614) and 100-DMA (1.1640), with the 200-DMA at 1.1412 as critical support. The MA alignment is transitioning from stacked bullish to tangled. Options structure shifted: 1M IV surged to ~11.5% from ~7.5%, placing it at the 80th percentile. Realised vol at ~12.5% exceeds implied, indicating underpricing of actual moves. Risk reversals at -0.6 vols reflect aggressive EUR put demand. The vol term structure is backwardated, pricing the super-week as dominant near-term binary event. Gamma is expensive at the front end but may be cheap relative to actual event risk. The structural levels that matter: 1.1412 (200-DMA), 1.1200 (December low), 1.1523 (March low), 1.1640/1.2019 as resistance.
key_data_points: 1M implied vol: ~11.5%, ~80th percentile 1Y
3M implied vol: ~10.0%, above 75th percentile
20d realised vol: ~12.5% — exceeds 1M IV by 1 point
25d risk reversal (1M): -0.6 vols (EUR puts bid)
Vol term structure: backwardation (pricing super-week)
200-DMA: 1.1412 (critical trend support)
tactical.what_is_priced: Options market is pricing elevated but not extreme risk — 1M IV at 80th percentile is expensive but not at panic levels (which would be >15%, >95th percentile). Risk reversal shift is meaningful but not extreme by 2022 standards. Market is pricing a moderate correction within the broader uptrend, not a structural reversal.

strategic.analysis: The strategic structure depends on whether the 200-DMA at 1.1412 holds. If it does, the broader uptrend from September 2024 remains intact and the current pullback is a healthy correction. Historical precedent supports this: EUR/USD tested its 200-DMA in October 2025 and bounced 4 cents. If the 200-DMA breaks, next support is 1.1200 and then 1.1000, with the structural trend questioned. Volatility should normalise from current elevated levels as geopolitical premium dissipates. The deeper liquidity of EUR/USD means structural levels are robust. The parity level is not in play on any reasonable scenario.
key_data_points: 200-DMA: 1.1412 (rising ~2 pips/day)
Uptrend intact: higher lows since Sep 2024 at 1.04, 1.07, 1.12
Historical 200-DMA tests: held Oct 2024 and Oct 2025
EUR/USD daily volume: ~$250bn notional
Strategic vol expectation: normalisation toward 8-9%
strategic.what_is_priced: Market is pricing a corrective phase within the broader uptrend. Base case is retest and hold of 200-DMA followed by gradual recovery toward 1.18-1.20. Market assigns ~15-20% probability to structural break lower.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical bearish (65%) vs strategic neutral (50%) reflects deteriorating near-term technicals vs the intact long-term uptrend. The strategic view depends entirely on the 200-DMA outcome.
factors.expression_assessment: The current vol landscape argues for nuance. With 1M IV at the 80th percentile and backwardated term structure, front-end options are expensive. However, RV exceeding IV suggests the market may still underprice actual moves. For the bearish tactical view: spot or short-dated put spreads are more efficient than outright puts given elevated IV. For the strategic bull case: selling front-end vol premium via put spreads or calendar spreads and buying longer-dated calls (3-6M) captures the term structure inversion while maintaining upside exposure. The cheapest expression of the divergent tactical/strategic view is a 1M/3M put ratio or calendar spread: sell expensive 1M vol, buy relatively cheaper 3M vol, and wait for event resolution.

tactical.analysis: USD funding markets have remained surprisingly orderly despite the geopolitical shock. SOFR at 3.65% is stable within the target range with no meaningful spread to IORB. EUR/USD 3M cross-currency basis remains near zero to modestly negative, indicating no acute dollar funding stress for European banks. ECB USD swap line usage is de minimis at $58 million. The Fed balance sheet at $6.6 trillion has stabilised since QT ended December 2025, with ~$40bn/month T-bill reserve management purchases. Reserve balances are well above the ~$3T minimum comfortable level. The ECB balance sheet at €6,293bn is normalising with excess reserves at ~€2,500bn. Schnabel estimated liquidity scarcity would not materialise before end-2026 at earliest. The Iran conflict introduces tail risk if the Hormuz closure triggers broader stress, but this has not materialised. The dollar smile places USD transitioning from exceptionalism to goldilocks, interrupted by the Iran safe-haven bid.
key_data_points: SOFR: 3.65% (stable within target range)
EUR/USD 3M cross-currency basis: near zero (no stress)
ECB USD swap line usage: $58 million (de minimis)
Fed balance sheet: $6.6 trillion (QT ended Dec 2025)
Reserve management purchases: ~$40bn/month T-bills
ECB balance sheet: €6,293bn; QT ~€500bn in 2026
Excess reserves: ~€2,500bn
tactical.what_is_priced: The market is pricing orderly USD funding conditions — no stress premium in basis, minimal swap line usage. The tail risk not priced is a prolonged Hormuz closure creating USD funding stress through the energy payment channel (European imports invoiced in USD). Probability of this: ~10-15%.

strategic.analysis: Over the strategic horizon, USD liquidity conditions broadly support EUR. The Fed's end of QT removes the systematic liquidity drain that was a background USD-positive. If the Fed cuts rates (base case: 1-2 cuts in H2), this directly eases global USD funding costs — SOFR declines, cross-currency basis improves, effective cost of USD for European banks falls. This is structurally EUR-positive by reducing demand-side support for the dollar. The ECB's continued normalisation (~€500bn QT) tightens EUR liquidity relative to USD, but excess reserves at €2,500bn mean this is gradual and absorbable. The dollar smile supports strategic EUR-bullish: the US economy is weakening (GDP +1.4% SAAR, NFP -92,000, unemployment 4.4%), moving USD away from the exceptionalism phase. In the absence of severe global recession, the transition to goldilocks or US concern territory is USD-negative and EUR-positive.
key_data_points: Fed QT: ended December 2025; balance sheet stable ~$6.6T
Fed rate path: 1-2 cuts expected in H2 2026
SOFR trajectory: expected decline toward 3.25-3.50% if Fed cuts
Dollar smile: USD transitioning from right (strong) to middle (weak)
US fiscal deficit: ~6-7% of GDP, structurally USD-negative
US NIIP: deeply negative (~-$20T)
strategic.what_is_priced: The market is pricing gradual USD weakening (consensus EUR/USD 1.20+ year-end). The edge is in the pace: if the US economy weakens faster than expected, the Fed would cut more aggressively and the dollar would weaken faster than priced. The risk is that the Iran conflict sustains safe-haven USD demand, delaying the smile transition by 1-2 quarters.

factors.horizon_split_explanation: Tactical neutral vs strategic bullish reflects near-term interruption of the dollar weakening trend by Iran-driven safe-haven demand vs structural forces (Fed easing, end of QT, dollar smile transition) that favour EUR over the strategic horizon.

weighted_score.interpretation: Net weighted score of -0.377 falls in the weak bearish range (-0.25 to -0.50). Six of ten factors are bearish, four are neutral, zero are bullish.
weighted_score.mapping_to_conviction: Weak bearish score maps to Conviction 2/5. Modest because the four neutral factors (including Policy & Rates at 0.24 weight) could swing in either direction at the March super-week.

tactical.rationale: The tactical EUR outlook is bearish with low conviction, driven by a confluence of five directionally aligned headwinds rather than a single dominant driver. The energy shock from the Iran conflict operates through multiple channels simultaneously: it worsens the external balance (Factor 5), elevates risk-off sentiment (Factor 3), threatens the growth recovery (Factor 2), and triggers positioning unwinds (Factor 4). The market structure has deteriorated with spot below the 50/100-DMA cluster and options skew flipping to EUR puts bid (Factor 9). Event risk (Factor 7) is the highest-confidence bearish call at 80%. However, conviction is limited to 2/5 because the largest-weight factor (Policy & Rates at 0.24) is neutral — the rate differential is simultaneously narrowing (structurally EUR-positive) and at risk of widening via ECB hike pricing (directionally ambiguous). The ECB meeting outcome is the single most important determinant: a hawkish hold with hike signalling would validate pricing and could paradoxically support EUR; a pushback would weaken EUR by signalling growth concern. The positioning overhang is the most mechanistically clear bearish driver at the 70th-85th percentile with 60,000-80,000 contracts of potential liquidation remaining. The path of least resistance is lower toward the 200-DMA at 1.1412. The risk to the bearish view is rapid Iran de-escalation combined with a dovish Fed, which would trigger a sharp risk-on rally and EUR/USD rebound toward 1.18+.

g10_ranking.commentary: EUR ranks near the bottom tactically because its anti-dollar character and energy import dependency make it among the most exposed G10 currencies to the current risk-off/energy shock regime. Only JPY (BoJ uncertainty) and CHF (zero-bound constraint) rank lower.

conditions: ECB holds with hawkish language but no explicit hike commitment
Iran conflict de-escalates gradually over 2-4 weeks
Positioning continues unwinding at 15,000-20,000 contracts per week
base_case.path: EUR/USD finds support at the 200-DMA (1.1412) and consolidates in a 1.14-1.17 range through early April.

conditions: Rapid Iran de-escalation with Hormuz reopening
Iran conflict escalates or persists beyond March
TTF exceeds €60/MWh and EU gas storage drops below 25%
Positioning cascade drives EUR/USD below the 200-DMA

tactical.path_expectation: The expected path over the next 2-4 weeks is a grind lower toward the 200-DMA at 1.1412, with elevated two-way volatility around the March 18-19 super-week. The pre-meeting drift should be EUR-negative as positioning unwinds and energy prices remain elevated. The super-week is a binary event that could produce a 150-250 pip move in either direction within 48 hours. Post-event, the path depends on the outcome: a dovish ECB accelerates the move toward 1.14, while a hawkish ECB/dovish Fed combination could trigger a reversal toward 1.18. The base case is that EUR/USD ends the tactical window in the 1.14-1.16 range.

increase_to_5_if: ECB hikes 50bp AND Fed cuts simultaneously
Iran conflict resolves AND EU gas storage rebounds above 50%
increase_to_4_if: ECB delivers hawkish hold validating hike path AND Fed signals imminent cuts
Iran de-escalation AND positioning reaches neutral territory
decrease_to_3_if: ECB delivers dovish hold disappointing hike expectations
EUR/USD breaks the 200-DMA at 1.1412 on a daily close
decrease_to_1_if: Rapid Iran de-escalation with Hormuz reopening
Fed delivers dovish surprise with explicit cut guidance for May

risk_reward.risk_reward_ratio: 0.69:1 (163 pip gain vs 237 pip risk) — marginally unfavourable
risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Slightly negative asymmetry — downside scenarios require multiple bearish catalysts to sustain, while the upside scenario (de-escalation) requires only one catalyst. This is consistent with the low conviction 2/5 rating.

weighted_score.interpretation: Net weighted score of +0.394 falls in the moderate bullish range (+0.25 to +0.50). Five factors bullish, five neutral, zero bearish.
weighted_score.mapping_to_conviction: Moderate bullish maps to Conviction 3/5. Bullish factors align on the structural thesis: rate convergence, fiscal expansion, ECB credibility, USD liquidity easing.

strategic.rationale: The strategic EUR outlook is bullish with moderate conviction, anchored by the alignment of three highest-weight factors: Policy & Rates (0.24, bullish), Guidance (0.12, bullish), and USD Liquidity (0.12, bullish). The UST-Bund 10Y spread has compressed from ~170bps to ~129bps over six months, and the structural forces driving compression — Fed easing, ECB at terminal, German fiscal expansion — remain intact. The spread is expected to narrow further to 100-110bps as the Fed delivers 1-2 cuts while the ECB holds or modestly tightens. Germany's €500bn infrastructure fund and defence expansion represent the most significant structural positive for the eurozone since the single currency's creation. DIW estimates +1% GDP in 2026. ECB credibility supports the narrative that the central bank will neither allow inflation to re-accelerate nor over-tighten into weakness. The dollar smile framework places USD in transition from exceptionalism to goldilocks, structurally EUR-positive. However, conviction is limited to 3/5 because the Iran energy shock introduces genuine uncertainty. Five factors are neutral rather than bullish, reflecting honest uncertainty from a live military conflict. The strategic bull case requires the base case (transitory energy shock) to materialise — it is conditional, not unconditional. The external balance at 0.16 strategic weight is neutral, and if it swings bearish on persistent energy disruption, it would be the most damaging factor given its weight.

g10_ranking.commentary: EUR ranks mid-table strategically, elevated from 9th tactically by the fiscal expansion narrative and rate convergence story. The improvement from 9th to 5th reflects the expectation that acute Iran-driven headwinds fade while structural tailwinds persist.

conditions: Iran conflict de-escalates, energy normalises ($80-90 Brent, €35-45 TTF)
Fed cuts once or twice in H2
German fiscal spending flows through procurement and infrastructure
base_case.path: Initial decline into Q2 (1.14-1.16) followed by gradual recovery from May/June as energy normalises and fiscal transmission begins.

conditions: Fed cuts 3x on US recession fears
German fiscal multiplier at high end (+2% GDP)
EU-US trade war escalates to autos

strategic.path_expectation: The expected path is an initial decline into Q2 (1.14-1.16 range) as the Iran shock works through the system, followed by a gradual recovery from May/June as energy prices normalise, the Fed begins cutting, and German fiscal spending flows through the economy. The path is front-loaded negative and back-loaded positive. The key inflection point is the resolution of the Hormuz closure — once energy prices normalise, the pre-conflict EUR bull trend should resume with the added tailwind of cheaper entry levels. By September 2026, the base case targets 1.19-1.22.

increase_to_5_if: Hormuz reopens AND Fed cuts 2+ times AND German fiscal GDP uplift exceeds 1%
Ukraine ceasefire AND EUR positioning overshoots to net short
increase_to_4_if: Hormuz reopens AND Fed signals cuts at March/May meeting
EUR positioning falls below 40th percentile creating contrarian long
decrease_to_3_if: Hormuz closure persists into May AND Brent sustained above $100
decrease_to_2_if: EU gas storage fails to reach 70% by June
decrease_to_1_if: Full energy crisis 2.0 with EU recession
ECB forced into aggressive hiking cycle of 100bp+

risk_reward.risk_reward_ratio: 0.85:1 (437 pip gain vs 513 pip risk) — near breakeven, consistent with moderate conviction
risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Slightly positive on the upside case: the 1.25 target (887 pips) is significantly larger than downside risk (513 pips), and structural forces supporting upside are more durable than the transitory shock driving downside.

consensus_divergence.consensus_view: The Reuters poll median forecasts EUR/USD at ~1.20 by year-end 2026. The bull consensus (Goldman, Deutsche Bank, BNP Paribas) targets 1.25, driven by structural USD weakness, German fiscal expansion, and Fed-ECB rate convergence. Most forecasts predate the Iran conflict.
consensus_divergence.our_view: We agree with the directional consensus (EUR/USD higher by year-end) but our confidence is lower (55% vs implied 65-70%) and our near-term path is more bearish. We expect EUR/USD to trade 1.14-1.16 for the next 4-8 weeks before recovering. Our year-end target range of 1.19-1.22 is consistent with the lower end of consensus.
consensus_divergence.divergence_magnitude: Moderate — ~1-2% below consensus median on a 6-month view but directionally aligned. Key divergence is in timing and path: we expect a deeper near-term trough (1.14-1.15) than consensus before recovery.
consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: We assign more weight to the positioning unwind dynamics (CFTC longs at 70th-85th percentile with 60,000-80,000 contracts of liquidation potential) and the energy shock's immediate terms of trade impact than consensus. Most bank forecasts predate the Iran conflict. We also believe ECB hike probability (~85% for two by December) is slightly overpriced, creating a near-term headwind if the ECB pushes back. Consensus underestimates path dependency: the EUR bull case requires the energy shock to resolve first.
consensus_divergence.payoff_if_right: A deeper-than-consensus trough (1.14-1.15) creates a superior entry point for the strategic long. Buying at 1.14-1.15 vs consensus timing of 1.16-1.18 adds 200-400 pips to the round-trip return.
consensus_divergence.risk_if_wrong: If the Iran conflict resolves faster than expected and the Fed delivers a dovish surprise, EUR/USD could snap directly to 1.18-1.20 without the deeper trough, causing our near-term bearish view to miss the move. Opportunity cost of waiting: ~200-400 pips of forgone appreciation.

cleanest_bullish_expression.rationale: EUR/CHF at 0.9039 captures the German fiscal expansion tailwind and rate differential widening (ECB 2.00% vs SNB 0.00%, +200bps carry) without USD noise from Iran/risk-off dynamics. The SNB is constrained at zero with near-deflationary Swiss inflation (0.1% YoY). The carry-to-vol ratio of +0.25 is the most attractive of any EUR cross. Target: 0.93-0.95 over 3-6 months.

cleanest_bearish_expression.rationale: Short EUR/GBP at 0.8652 captures GBP's higher carry (+175bps over EUR), the BoE's less aggressive easing path due to sticky 3.0% UK CPI, and widened Gilt-Bund spread at 166-188bps. EUR/GBP is relatively insulated from USD/risk-off dynamics. Target: 0.8500-0.8550 over 2-4 weeks.

relative_value_pairs.thesis: Long EUR/JPY at 183.36 as a relative value expression of European fiscal expansion vs Japanese structural challenges, with +125bps carry in EUR's favour and BoJ's hiking cycle already well-priced. Strategic (3-6M) expression given JPY safe-haven demand near-term.

cross_horizon_consistency.summary: The tactical and strategic stances diverge: tactically bearish (conviction 2/5) vs strategically bullish (conviction 3/5).
cross_horizon_consistency.divergence_explanation: The near-term energy shock, positioning unwind, and risk-off sentiment are transitory forces dominating the 2-4 week horizon but should dissipate over 1-6 months. The structural forces — German fiscal expansion, Fed-ECB rate convergence, USD liquidity easing, ECB credibility — are durable and assert themselves once transitory headwinds fade. This is a classic short the correction, buy the dip setup.
cross_horizon_consistency.inflection_point: April-May 2026, triggered by Iran conflict de-escalation, ECB March meeting resolution, positioning reaching neutral, and German fiscal spending flowing through data
cross_horizon_consistency.catalyst_for_transition: Resolution of the Hormuz closure is the single most important catalyst. Once energy prices normalise ($80-90 Brent, €35-45 TTF), the pre-conflict EUR bull narrative reasserts immediately.
cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: Tactically underweight EUR or hedge exposure through 3-4 weeks using short-dated put spreads. Strategically accumulate EUR longs at lower levels (1.14-1.15 target zone) with a 6-month target of 1.20-1.22.

edge.1m: -125 pips (forward implies 1.1625, we expect 1.1500) — forward overstates EUR
edge.3m: -101 pips (forward implies 1.1651, we expect 1.1550) — forward overstates EUR
edge.6m: +305 pips (forward implies 1.1695, we expect 1.2000) — forward understates EUR

forward_vs_spot.interpretation: EUR/USD forward points are positive across the curve reflecting the USD-EUR rate differential. At 1M and 3M, forwards overstate our expected EUR performance — we expect EUR to weaken before recovering. This creates a negative edge at the front end. At 6M, the forward at 1.1695 significantly understates our expected 1.2000, creating a 305-pip edge representing the core strategic opportunity. Buying EUR/USD 6M forward at 1.1695 captures both carry and expected appreciation for ~5.2% annualised return.

key_catalysts.rationale: BoJ expected to hold at 0.75%. A surprise hike would strengthen JPY broadly and weaken EUR/JPY, but EUR/USD impact is indirect. A hold is consensus and should not move EUR/USD.

key_catalysts.event: Federal Reserve FOMC Decision + Dot Plot
key_catalysts.rationale: Fed expected to hold, but dot plot update will reveal assessment of -92,000 NFP and rising unemployment. A dovish pivot (more dots pointing to cuts) would weaken USD and support EUR/USD. A hawkish hold would be EUR-negative.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Partially — market expects hold with modest dovish tilt; aggressive shift would surprise

key_catalysts.event: ECB Governing Council Decision + Staff Projections
key_catalysts.rationale: March meeting with updated projections is the most important EUR event. A hawkish hold with hikes on the table would validate pricing and support EUR. A pushback against hike expectations would weaken EUR. The projections recalibrate the entire outlook.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Partially — ~85% probability of two hikes by December priced; direction of surprise depends on delivery

key_catalysts.rationale: BoE expected to hold at 3.75%. A surprise cut would weaken GBP and strengthen EUR/GBP. A hawkish hold on sticky CPI would be modestly EUR/GBP-negative.
SNB expected to hold at 0.00%. With Swiss CPI at 0.1%, the SNB faces the prospect of returning to negative rates. Any signal of discomfort with CHF strength would be EUR/CHF-positive.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Partially — hold expected, forward guidance uncertain

key_catalysts.rationale: Agenda includes Ukraine, defence, competitiveness, MFF. Concrete progress on defence spending or Ukraine diplomacy would be EUR-positive. Likely framework-setting rather than market-moving.

First read on business confidence post-Iran conflict. A sharp decline from 51.9 composite would confirm growth damage from the energy shock. A resilient reading above 51 would be surprisingly EUR-positive.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Partially — market expects some deterioration but magnitude uncertain

key_catalysts.rationale: March HICP captures the first month of post-Iran energy prices. Headline could jump to 2.3-2.5% or higher. A large overshoot reinforces ECB hike expectations and creates a stagflationary narrative.
EU tariffs on ~$28bn of US goods take effect, risking counter-retaliation. Escalation to auto tariffs would be significantly EUR-negative. Even without escalation, tariffs reduce trade volumes.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Partially — tariffs announced but counter-retaliation risk not priced

key_catalysts.rationale: No updated projections, but statement and press conference will reveal whether March hawkish tilt has intensified or faded with energy developments.

June projections incorporate 3 months of post-Iran data and emerging fiscal transmission. If conflict has de-escalated and growth is improving, projections should upgrade GDP, supporting strategic bull case. If a hike is delivered, it would be at this meeting.
key_catalysts.priced_in: Partially — ~55% probability of first hike by June

key_catalysts.rationale: EU-US trade deal review could de-escalate tensions (EUR-positive) or trigger broader tariff actions including autos (significantly EUR-negative). Outcome depends on political dynamics and Iran resolution.

AfD leads polls by 10+ points in both states and could win its first Minister-President. While not directly affecting federal policy, it signals political fragmentation that could raise concerns about fiscal expansion execution.

invalidators.condition: Brent crude sustains above $110/bbl for more than 4 weeks
invalidators.mechanism: Prolonged energy disruption erodes the current account surplus, forces ECB hawkishness into weakness, and recreates the 2022 energy crisis dynamic.

invalidators.condition: ECB hikes 50bp or more at a single meeting
invalidators.mechanism: Aggressive front-loading signals persistent inflation risk overwhelming growth considerations, tightening financial conditions into a nascent energy shock.

invalidators.condition: EUR/USD closes below 1.1200 on a weekly basis
invalidators.mechanism: Confirms structural trend reversal from the 2024-2026 bull market, breaking the pattern of higher lows and triggering systematic selling.

invalidators.condition: CFTC non-commercial positioning flips to net short below -20,000 contracts
invalidators.mechanism: Capitulation in EUR positioning creates conditions for a contrarian long with structural fundamentals intact.

invalidators.condition: US NFP rebounds above +200,000 and unemployment falls below 4.2%
invalidators.mechanism: Invalidates the US weakening narrative that underpins Fed easing expectations and the dollar smile transition.

invalidators.condition: TTF natural gas exceeds €80/MWh and EU gas storage falls below 20%
invalidators.mechanism: Full-blown energy crisis comparable to 2022, requiring emergency measures and fundamentally undermining the eurozone growth outlook.
invalidators.monitor_indicator: ICE TTF front-month and AGSI+ storage data

invalidators.mechanism: Removes the largest geopolitical risk premium in European assets, reduces energy prices, and unlocks a peace dividend of improved confidence.

signposts.threshold_confirms: Spot holds above on weekly close (uptrend intact)
signposts.threshold_invalidates: Spot closes below on consecutive weekly basis (trend reversal)

signposts.threshold_confirms: Refill pace reaches 1.5%/week by May (on track)
signposts.threshold_invalidates: Falls below 20% or refill pace below 0.5%/week by June

seasonality.pattern: March-April ECB projection effect: EUR/USD shows elevated volatility around March ECB meetings with updated staff projections. In 6 of the last 10 years, EUR/USD moved more than 2% in the 2 weeks surrounding the March meeting. June HICP base effects: favourable energy anniversary effects could mechanically push headline below 2.0%.
seasonality.historical_magnitude: 2%+ EUR/USD move in 2 weeks around March ECB meeting (6 of 10 years); June base effects can shift headline HICP by 0.3-0.5pp
seasonality.current_alignment: The March meeting seasonality is directly relevant with the 18-19 March decision. The June base effect is conditionally relevant — it would reinforce the disinflation narrative if the energy shock has normalised by then.

assumptions: Energy shock duration: base case assumes Strait of Hormuz disruption resolved within 2-4 weeks, with energy prices settling at moderately elevated levels ($80-90 Brent, €35-45 TTF) rather than returning to pre-conflict levels or escalating further.
CFTC data lag: latest available data is as of 3 March. Actual positioning as of 10 March is estimated ~20,000-30,000 contracts lower, placing net specs at approximately 110,000-115,000 contracts.
PPP estimates: OECD purchasing power parity estimates used as primary valuation benchmark. PPP is a long-run equilibrium concept and does not imply short-term mean-reversion.
Carry calculations: simple policy rate differential (EUR deposit rate vs peer policy rate). Actual carry depends on FX swap points, cross-currency basis, and funding costs.
Volatility estimates: 1M and 3M implied vol figures estimated from most recent available data and adjusted directionally. Exact IV levels may differ from real-time quotes.
BIS REER data published with 1-2 month lag. February 2026 is latest available. Percentile ranking estimated from historical distribution since 2000.
Macro comparison table uses most recently available official statistics with potentially different reference periods across countries.
